edge: OWOD bridges the gap between classic object detection benchmarks and object detection in the real world.
In standard OD, object proposals not overlapping with a labeled object are automatically classified as background, which poses a challenge in OWOD.
OWOD algorithms are expected to detect novel/unknown objects, which is a key aspect of the task.
The challenge of detecting unknown objects stems from the lack of supervision in distinguishing unknown objects and background object proposals.
Previous OWOD methods have attempted to overcome the issue of unknown object detection by generating supervision using pseudo-labeling.
Probabilistic/generative models may provide a solution for the challenge of detecting unknown objects in OWOD.
The introduced probabilistic framework for objectness estimation alternates between probability distribution estimation and objectness likelihood maximization.
PROB integrates the novel probabilistic framework into traditional object detection models, adapting them for the open-world scenario.

Open World Learning (OWL) is a new paradigm of learning that facilitates the development of machine learning methods which maintain their robustness in the real world.
Open World Learning (OWL) tasks a machine learning system with reasoning about both known and unknown concepts while learning over time from a non-stationary data stream.
In Open World Object Detection (OWOD), a model is expected to detect all previously learned objects and novel unknown objects simultaneously.
Unknown object detection is pivotal to the objective of Open World Object Detection (OWOD).

The objectness head is designed to predict the probability of an object being present in the query.
If the query contains an object, the objectness prediction should be high, and the task of classifying the query into any known objects or an unknown object is left to the classification head.
To build a robust objectness model, probabilistic models are used where the objectness probability is parametrized as a multivariate Gaussian distribution in the query embedding space.
The Mahalanobis distance is used as a measure for the query embeddings to calculate the objectness likelihood.
Class-agnostic Gaussian distribution is used to model the embedded feature space for learning general object features shared across all classes.
The training process involves estimating the distribution parameters in an alternating, two-step process.

Estimating the distribution parameters and maximizing the likelihood of matched embeddings involve estimating the batch-mean, µ ∈ RD, and covariance, Σ ∈ RD×D of all the query embeddings Q using the empirical mean and covariance estimators.
To maximize the likelihood of matched embeddings, the squared Mahalanobis distance, dM (qi)2, of matched query embeddings is penalized.
The objectness loss, Lo = dM (qi)2, is defined by the squared Mahalanobis distance of matched query embeddings.
In the OWOD objective, models are expected to incrementally learn newly discovered objects without catastrophically forgetting previously seen objects.
OWOD methods keep a small set of images, or exemplars, to mitigate catastrophic forgetting.
Actively selecting instances based on their objectness score has the potential to further improve OWOD performance.

Instances with low objectness are expected to be relatively difficult instances, as the model was unsure of whether they were an object, and learning them is expected to improve the model performance on newly introduced objects.
Meanwhile, instances with high objectness are expected to be highly representative of that object class.
The selection of instances with high objectness is expected to impede catastrophic forgetting, as shown in the incremental learning field.
This is in line with current state-of-the-art active learning methods [28].
Specifically, after training on a particular dataset Dt, we compute the objectness probability of every matched query embedding. We then select the top/bottom 25 scoring objects per object class.
We performed extensive experiments on all OWOD benchmarks, comparing PROB to all reported OWOD methods (Sec. 5.1).
Extensive ablations show the importance of each one of PROB’s components while shedding additional light on their function (Sec. 5.2).
Finally, we test our model’s incremental learning performance on the PASCAL VOC 2007 benchmark compared to other OWOD and incremental learning methods (Sec. 5.3).
